title: Effect Dependencies and Cleanup

learningObjectives.text: Explain dependency arrays using a new example and justify the result.
Explain stale dependencies using a new example and justify the result.
Explain cleanup functions using a new example and justify the result.
Explain avoiding leaks and races using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns effect dependencies and cleanup from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Effects and Hooks** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Dependency arrays** → **Stale dependencies** → **Cleanup functions** → **Avoiding leaks and races**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Dependency arrays
sections.body: An Effect's dependency list includes every reactive value used by its code. React reruns the Effect when a dependency changes.

sections.title: Stale dependencies
sections.body: Omitting a reactive dependency can make an Effect read values captured by an older render.

sections.title: Cleanup functions
sections.body: Cleanup reverses setup by unsubscribing, removing listeners, aborting work, or clearing timers before rerun and on unmount.

sections.title: Avoiding leaks and races
sections.body: Long-lived subscriptions and overlapping async work require cleanup or cancellation so stale work does not update current state.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: Complete dependencies and abort cleanup prevent stale overlapping work.

commonMistakes: Suppressing the dependency linter instead of fixing the data flow.
Using an Effect for render-time derivation.
Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
summary: Effect Dependencies and Cleanup connects Dependency arrays, Stale dependencies, Cleanup functions.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Dependency arrays” in the context of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup?

choices.label: A component treats props as immutable snapshots for a render. Mutating them creates hidden coupling with the owner.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Dependency arrays.

choices.label: Class components use instance state, this, and lifecycle methods. They matter for legacy code but are not the preferred model for new teaching.

choices.label: A Redux store holds the application state tree and coordinates dispatch, reducers, subscriptions, and middleware.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Dependency arrays.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

choices.label: Apply Dependency arrays deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Effect Dependencies and Cleanup?

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Dependency arrays, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Effect Dependencies and Cleanup?

choices.label: Use Effect Dependencies and Cleanup when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

choices.label: Use Effect Dependencies and Cleanup in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

choices.label: Avoid Effect Dependencies and Cleanup completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Effect Dependencies and Cleanup.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
